The ever-shrinking time window

Different technical approaches are accelerating simultaneously and mutually validating each other.

data.title: The Overrated Individual: A Survival Guide to China's "One-Person Companies"
data.description: In late 2024, Israeli developer Maor Shlomo, discharged from the reserves, opened his laptop, and began working on a project. He had no funding, no team, and no Slack channel. Six months later, Wix acquired his company, Base44, for $80 million in cash. At that time, the product already had 250,000 users and a monthly profit of $189,000. In the three months before the acquisition, he hadn't written a single line of front-end code. The story of Dutchman Pieter Levels is even more remarkable. One person, zero employees, using only basic PHP and jQuery, simultaneously running three products: Nomad List, Remote OK, and Photo AI. In 2022, his total revenue had already reached $2.7 million. He had never held a job, never raised funds, and lived a digital nomadic life in over 40 countries and 150 cities. These stories are so compelling, they create the illusion that one person and a computer can build a skyscraper from scratch. Incubators for one-person companies are springing up everywhere in Shenzhen, Shanghai, Suzhou, and Hangzhou. Nanshan Moli Camp, with 100,000 square meters, has attracted 700 companies to apply; Lingang Zero Boundary Magic Cube offers free workstations, and the first phase of 300 workstations was snapped up, with the second phase of 8,000 square meters already underway. Dan Shao, who runs a one-person company community in Chengdu, has seen every event fully booked in less than a month. The trend has truly exploded. According to the "China OPC Development Trend Report (2025-2030)," as of June 2025, the number of one-person limited liability companies nationwide had exceeded 16 million. In the first half of 2025, the number of newly registered OPCs nationwide reached 2.86 million, a surge of 47% year-on-year, accounting for 23.8% of all newly registered enterprises. But what lies beneath this trend? We spoke with three people on this path: an observer who has been operating a one-person company community for nearly two years and has over 2,500 real-world samples; a Gen Z entrepreneur who has run two companies across Silicon Valley and the US; and an independent developer who transitioned from a primary market financial advisor to an AI agent product developer. Their stories are quite different from those on social media. The Underlying Logic of Success Dai Wenqian runs SoloNest, a one-person company community in Shanghai, for a simple reason. In June 2024, she had just left the online education industry and wanted to know what a person could actually do. Unable to find answers in books and videos, she decided to conduct her own field research: organizing meetings, examining samples, and conducting interviews. In nearly two years, she accumulated over 2,500 samples, 20% of which successfully established a business model and yielded results. She also wrote a book based on her observations of these samples: *One-Person Companies*. A joint study by Qichacha and Xiaobaochao shows that the three-year survival rate of companies established in China in 2021 has fallen to 71%, with nearly a quarter failing within the first three years. Clearly, SoloNest's 20% success rate has outperformed the market. But Dai Wenqian is more concerned about the 80%. "Those who didn't succeed fall into two categories. One type tried but failed, either due to a lack of traffic or an unsustainable model. The other type never even started," she analyzed. The number of people who didn't even begin is far greater than imagined. "Because he hasn't experienced enough pain, he has a way out. His brain deceives him into thinking he wants to start, but essentially he's just afraid of missing out. Most of them are worried about their current jobs and feel that OPC entrepreneurship might be a solution, but fear-driven motivation can be a terrible starting point," said Dai Wenqian. Leon, who has worked as a financial advisor in the primary market, has seen many more people like this. He attended offline events for one-person companies and found that many people lacked direction, attending events everywhere and buying courses indiscriminately. "No one can help you find out how to make money. The only correct path is to do it, to learn from mistakes, and to suffer losses," Leon said. Of those who started, who survived? The answer is very counterintuitive. Dai Wenqian observed a consistent commonality in the effective sample: those who successfully navigated the closed loop almost never worked in their original industry. They didn't choose their path based on "what I'm good at," but rather on "where there are unmet needs." Dai Wenqian used herself as an example. Previously, she worked on branding at Himalaya FM, without experience in offline events or community building. However, she possessed a curiosity about people, an aesthetic sense for product design, and the ability to express herself in a structured way—fundamental abilities that transcend industry limitations. Once she identified market demands, scenarios, and industry pain points, she could transfer these fundamental abilities. There's also a guy in the SoloNest community who makes tennis bags; he didn't originally make bags either. Because of his love for tennis, he discovered a need that wasn't being met by existing products on the market. He then invested 100,000 yuan in startup capital to create an original tennis bag. After two years, it now sells a stable 300+ bags a month. This has a precise counterpart in Pieter Levels' methodology. In 2014, Levels set himself a challenge: to create 12 products in 12 months and see which ones would resonate with the market. Nomad List was the 7th, and the only one to succeed. The key isn't just choosing the right path, but also quickly validating enough hypotheses. Dai Wenqian breaks this process down into three watershed moments. The first is whether you dare to create something by hand and launch it into the market. Unfortunately, many people don't even have the mindset for validation; they just think about it but don't act. The second hurdle is whether you can sell it after someone shows interest. There's a huge gap between "someone thinks it's good" and "someone keeps paying for it." The third hurdle is whether you can free yourself from the delivery process. The first two hurdles filter out those who are inactive or do nothing. The third hurdle is the real challenge. Those who pass the first two hurdles will find that they are alive, but there's a ceiling firmly welded to their heads. Dai Wenqian gave a precise figure: the annual revenue ceiling for an individual relying solely on delivery is approximately 1 million to 1.2 million RMB. “No matter how hard you work, there’s a limit to how much you can sell your time,” she said. This is the most realistic dilemma facing one-person companies in China. Social media talks about Maor Shlomo’s $80 million exit and Pieter Levels’ $2.7 million annual revenue, but those are stories of Silicon Valley SaaS and globalized digital products. In China, one-person companies thrive more in the consumer market, the service industry, and the experience economy, with heavy delivery chains and people tied together. Barry, who has done business across China and the US, has seen a more fundamental difference. American entrepreneurs think about B2B SaaS and AI agents. Chinese entrepreneurs think about physical industries like pets, elderly care, and food. This isn’t about who’s smarter; it’s about the difference in industrial structure and willingness to pay between the two countries. American companies are highly willing to pay for services; a small SaaS tool can easily get it working. The B2B ecosystem in China is completely different. So, how do we break through the 100,000-200,000 ceiling? The most intuitive path is automation—using AI to free itself from the delivery chain. But this path is far more difficult than the narrative suggests. There's a typical example in the SoloNest community. Jason's business is job hunting coaching, helping interns and recent graduates in internet operations revise their resumes, conduct mock interviews, and assist with job hunting. He started by selling his time, taking on a dozen clients a month. Many competitors get stuck here and fail. Jason's approach is to find a group of competitors who struggle to acquire customers consistently, train them, and refer clients to them. Payment is split per deal, without an employer-employee relationship. Dai Wenqian calls this "multiple one-person companies collaborating, not one multi-person company." Later, he expanded into B2B outsourced operations, transforming from a purely C-end to a C+B model. Now Jason is working on the third step: building a knowledge base using consulting data from the past two years to create a semi-automated delivery product. But after two months, he's only completed 60%. Why is it so slow? Dai Wenqian provided a mathematical model: "Suppose your delivery chain has 5 key nodes, and each node, through automation, can only achieve 80% of the performance of manual work. Does that mean the overall pass rate after automation is 80%? No, it might be 0.8 x 0.8 x 0.8 x 0.8 x 0.8, only 33%. The longer the chain, the harder automation becomes. It's not an additive relationship, it's a multiplicative one." This is why lobsters seem like they can be automated "all at once," but once you actually build one, you realize that if any link in the chain isn't done well, the result is garbage. The prerequisite for using AI effectively is that you've already done a good job manually; otherwise, you won't be able to see where the problems are. Leon has the strongest technical foundation among the three interviewees. He now develops his own AI Agent product, without writing a single line of code; all development is handled by AI. AI penetration in his workflow is close to 100%. However, he was very restrained in his assessment of automation: "To evaluate whether a task can be handed over to AI, I look at three points: how low the cost is, how high the risk is, and how good the effect is. Services for high-net-worth individuals cannot be handled by AI. AI works by allowing it to make mistakes; it optimizes its strategy through those mistakes. But in providing services to high-net-worth individuals, mistakes are not allowed. If you make a mistake in communication, the entire business is ruined." Some business processes simply cannot be replaced by AI. Dai Wenqian herself also admits that her AI penetration rate is only 30%. This is because her core delivery involves offline, person-to-person interaction, which cannot be automated. She can only perform partial automation, including content acquisition and knowledge base accumulation, but she cannot completely detach herself from the business. She works more than 14 hours a day. Her tasks include creating content to attract new users, chatting with and filtering contacts, maintaining partnerships, designing products, and disassembling samples. She also has two scheduled offline events every weekend. "Many one-person company founders wouldn't post this kind of thing online. Nobody sees it. Everyone likes to see glamorous images: drinking coffee here, attending exhibitions there, earning millions a year, a successful businesswoman. But the reality is, entrepreneurship involves a lot of dirty and tiring work, constant repetition, and constant iteration," she said. One-person companies are not the end. Automation is one path, but not the only one. Dai Wenqian observed another breakthrough approach: not by replacing oneself, but by piecing oneself together. Jason's case exemplifies this logic. He doesn't hire people, but collaborates with other one-person companies. Each Lego piece is an independent entity, each with its own capabilities and customers, creating incremental growth when pieced together. Furthermore, if every one-person company can be enhanced by AI, then piecing them together creates enhanced Lego. Dai Wenqian believes this is the greatest source of imagination for one-person companies: "It's like Lego; not every Lego piece needs to be 100% AI-enabled, but each Lego piece is enhanced by AI. Three enhanced Lego pieces together aren't 1+1+1, but 3×3×3." Another path is to replicate one's experience and methodology to more people. Barry's experience validated this model. He was the founder of two one-man companies, exploring everything from scratch. Once the business model was established, he stepped back and handed it over to the team, allowing them to take over like a relay race, while he moved on to other businesses. Maor Shlomo made a similar choice. Base44 grew to 250,000 users and nearly $200,000 in monthly profit within six months, but he still chose to sell it to Wix. His explanation was that despite the phenomenal growth, the scale and size needed couldn't be organically achieved by one person. One person can take a product from 0 to 1, but going from 1 to 100 requires organization, resources, and distribution capabilities—something one person cannot do alone. Three different paths: AI productization, collaborative integration, and partner expansion, but the underlying logic is the same: a one-man company is not the final state. It's a springboard. Once you've successfully validated something at the lowest cost, you must find a way to move beyond that bottleneck. Otherwise, you'll be forever stuck on that 1.2 million line. Before the door closes... The data for 2026 is impressive. Shenzhen issued the OPC Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Action Plan, aiming to build over 10 OPC communities of 10,000 square meters each by 2027. Shanghai Pudong provides up to 300,000 yuan worth of free computing power to newly registered one-person companies. Suzhou attracted 300,000 university students in 2025, and its talent pool is rapidly expanding. But Dai Wenqian made a sobering remark. "The barrier to entry has been greatly lowered. Previously, finding funding, people, and venues was extremely costly. Now you can validate almost anything at zero cost. But this benefits everyone equally; if it's easy for you, it's easy for everyone else. With more players, traffic becomes more expensive. It's an arms race." Pieter Levels can earn $2.7 million a year because he started in 2014, accumulating a decade of SEO expertise and community trust. Maor Shlomo of Base44 was able to sell his company in six months because he had previously run a data company that raised $125 million; his network, judgment, and speed weren't built from scratch. These people aren't the "ordinary people can do it too" narrative often told in one-person companies. They are among the brightest points of survivorship bias. The real world of one-person companies is exemplified by the 2,500+ samples in the SoloNest community: 20% are consistently making money and moving to the next stage; 40% are stuck in various ways but are still iterating and trying to break through; and another 40% are still lost and searching for direction. Of the surviving 20%, most earn less than 1.2 million annually. They work until the early hours of the morning, their weekdays are packed, and they have no weekends. In fact, the one-person company business profits from the time difference between "a niche demand being discovered but not yet being occupied by organized capital." This time difference has a name: shelf life. Shelf life depends on two things: the timing of your discovery of the demand and the speed at which you implement it. Lowering the barrier to entry won't lengthen the shelf life. On the contrary, it will shorten it. Because what you can validate at zero cost, others can also validate at zero cost. The needs you see, others can also see. If you create an MVP today and it survives for three months, tomorrow ten identical products will appear on the same user's phone. This is why most people get "stuck." The essence of being stuck isn't a lack of ability, but rather that the speed at which time is realized for them can't keep up with the speed of market congestion. The reason Maor Shlomo and Pieter Levels are advertisements rather than examples is precisely because they solved the shelf life problem in two opposite ways. Levels extended its shelf life to ten years through first-mover advantage and compound interest, while Shlomo compressed it to six months through speed and exit strategies. The middle path is the most dangerous. For most founders of one-person companies in China, there's neither ten years to slowly build a flywheel, nor a Wix to write checks. They work 14 hours a day, maintaining a ceiling of 1.2 million, thinking that if they just hold on a little longer, they can break through. But the market won't wait for you to hold on. The next competitor with zero-cost validation can appear at any time, flattening your little moat. A one-person company is never a state you can stay in for long. It's a window with a shelf life. When the window is open, the barriers to entry are low, tools are cheap, and the demand is clear—it seems like the best of times for ordinary people. But the window won't stay open forever. It will be filled with newcomers, crushed by more efficient tools, and finally shut down completely by a startup that receives funding or a large company's business line that suddenly exits the market. The only real challenge in this business is whether you can remove yourself from that bottleneck position before it closes.

data.title: Crypto’s Casualty List Grows: 86 Projects Fail as Billions Flow to ETFs and Stablecoins
data.description:
2026 has not been a good year for crypto companies as more and more projects are finding it harder and harder to make ends meet.

In just the first quarter of the new year, 86 high-profile projects—spanning wallets, NFTs, DeFi platforms, and governance tools—shuttered, while capital shifts toward regulated and institutional channels signal the end of crypto’s easy-money era.

More than 20 crypto projects have shut down in early 2026, as weaker business models struggle to survive in a tighter, post-bull market environment.

Closures span DeFi, NFTs, wallets, and gaming, driven by falling activity, shrinking liquidity, and rising costs. pic.twitter.com/mZeGqTG4IQ

— BitDigest.io (@BitDigestdaily) April 6, 2026

Iconic apps crumble under economic reality

RootData’s “dead project” archive reports 86 closures by March 20. Leading casualties include Magic Eden, which will sunset its wallet by May 1, and Gemini-owned Nifty Gateway, now withdrawal-only. Decentralized email provider Dmail, DeFi platform Balancer Labs, and DAO governance tool Tally also signaled closure.

🚨JUST IN: MAGIC EDEN TO SHUTDOWN BITCOIN & EVM MARKETPLACES@MagicEden will close down its Bitcoin and EVM marketplaces, according to a report from Blockspace, citing 'sources close to the matter'.

Thereafter, the platform is expected to wind down its cross-chain wallet… pic.twitter.com/0x2nL45YSv

— BSCN (@BSCNews) February 27, 2026

However, many of these "failures" didn't just happen overnight. Most were incubated during the 2021–2022 crypto mania or the 2024–2025 rebound, relying on explosive user growth, token incentives, and easy-flowing venture capital. When activity slowed and volumes declined, the fragile economies collapsed.

DeFi analyst Ignas noted that Crypto’s easy money era has ended as every model built for speculative gains has been fully exploited.

According to him, crypto's run, beginning with the initial coin offering craze of 2017 and rolling through DeFi summer, the NFT mania, airdrops, point farming,and memecoin speculation, has lasted for more than 8 years.

Flight to institutional quality reshapes crypto

Capital hasn’t left crypto—it has simply migrated to more secure, regulated avenues. US spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded $1.32 billion inflows in March, ending a four-month streak of outflows. Stablecoins now hover at a $300 billion market cap, backed by major institutions like Fidelity and Western Union. Tokenized real-world assets tracked by RWA.xyz surpassed $26 billion, attracting BNP Paribas, BlackRock, and other financial heavyweights.

Speculative apps are left starving as institutional rails capture liquidity. Bitcoin ETFs siphon retail and institutional demand into regulated portfolios. Stablecoins dominate payment, settlement, and treasury flows. Tokenized Treasuries offer yield within transparent, compliant frameworks.

🇺🇸 Blackrock ETF has bought $181,880,000 in Bitcoin.

Have you ever wondered what they know? pic.twitter.com/XYFAE6wNH7

— Bitcoin professor (@Bitcoinprof0637) April 7, 2026

The message is clear: crypto is consolidating around products that demonstrate real-world utility, compliance, and recurring revenue. Apps that relied solely on hype or cultural relevance are failing to survive.

Survival now demands infrastructure and users

The baseline for success has shifted. General-purpose wallets and NFT apps cannot rely on fleeting attention or viral hype. Long-term survival requires robust infrastructure, repeated user engagement, and sustainable revenue models. As Ignas frames it:

“What’s left to earn requires real infra, real users, real revenue.”

Q1 2026’s carnage clears the field for specialist projects bridging crypto and traditional finance. The long tail is collapsing, the head is consolidating, and the era of the crypto casino has ended—ushering in a more disciplined, institutional-grade market.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Investors have two major misjudgments regarding the situation in Iran.
data.description: The U.S. economy is not as immune to the energy shocks caused by war as many investors believe. Michael Cembalest, Chairman of Markets and Investment Strategy at JPMorgan Asset and Wealth Management, pointed out in a new report released this Monday that two widely accepted but fundamentally flawed assessments circulate in the market regarding the conflict with Iran: First, the U.S. economy can withstand the impact of a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz due to its energy independence; second, the pressure from Iran reopening the strait is enough to force a rapid de-escalation of the situation. Cembalest believes both assessments are overly optimistic. The report was released as Trump's latest deadline for Iran to immediately reopen the Strait of Hormuz expires Tuesday evening. Meanwhile, the decline in the US stock market during this conflict has been relatively limited, which some investors interpret as a signal of market "immunity" to the situation. However, Cembalest's analysis suggests that this calm may be based on a systemic underestimation of risk. Misconception 1: US Energy Independence Can Withstand External Shocks. Cembalest directly addresses this market consensus in his report: "The claim that the US is immune to the impact of a Strait of Hormuz blockade is fundamentally wrong. US fossil fuel independence does not constitute an economic firewall as you might imagine." This conclusion is supported not by theoretical deduction, but by the actual market movements. While the outside world generally focuses on the risks faced by European and Asian countries due to the Strait of Hormuz blockade, the reality is that the price increases of various refined petroleum products, and even crude oil itself, have been more significant in the US market. This means that even though the United States is a net exporter of certain fuels, a sharp rise in global energy prices will still be transmitted to the US domestic market through market mechanisms, having a substantial impact on consumers and businesses. Misconception Two: Iran Will Be Forced to Make Quick Concessions. The second misconception is that some market participants believe that US military pressure and economic costs will force Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz as soon as possible. Cembalest holds reservations about this. In his report, he cites the views of Bloomberg Middle East economist Dina Esfandiary, who points out that Iran has realized that the strategy of holding the global economy hostage is less costly and more effective than expected. In other words, Iran has concluded from the current situation that this strategy has unexpectedly worked. Cembalest also lists several structural factors that make it difficult for the situation to end quickly. First, even if the Strait of Hormuz reopens tomorrow, oil production in the region will take time to recover to pre-conflict levels. Second, the interceptor missile stockpiles of the United States, Israel, and Gulf states may be running low. Furthermore, Iran's significant progress in drone manufacturing has greatly enhanced its ability to conduct asymmetric warfare. Cembalest wrote in his report, "While drones have small payloads, they can inflict enormous damage on much more expensive aircraft, ships, and radar systems with just a small payload, and the payload per unit cost of a drone is higher than that of many missile systems." The US Navy's minesweeping capabilities are also a concern—currently, the fleet has only four aging minesweepers, all of which are scheduled for retirement. Despite the accumulating risks mentioned above, the US stock market has remained relatively stable in this conflict, with a significantly smaller decline than the historic shocks of last year's tariff war, the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict, and the early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic. In an interview with MarketWatch, Stephanie Link, chief investment strategist at Hightower Advisors, said the resilience of U.S. stocks was "fascinating," attributing it to two main factors: upward revisions to earnings forecasts by Wall Street analysts and a robust U.S. labor market. However, Link also cautioned about tail risks: "If the conflict lasts longer than a few months, I think the impact on the market and the U.S. economy will certainly be more severe." Cembalest, in the opening of his report, used Stephen King's novel *Salem's Town* as a metaphor, suggesting that the current situation may unfold very differently from initial expectations—the protagonist goes to fight evil with good intentions, only to have the town razed to the ground and everyone's situation worsened. This metaphor may be his most concise assessment of the entire situation in Iran.
